coredata.dc:title: A label-free and carbon dots based fluorescent aptasensor for the detection of kanamycin in milk
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jinlong
Lu, Tingting
Hu, Yang
Wang, Xueli
Wu, Yuangen
coredata.dc:description: A novel label-free aptasensor for kanamycin detection was constructed using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as absorber to quench the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) via the inner filter effect (IFE). The strategy was mainly relied on the fact that the absorption spectra of AuNPs overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectra of fluorophores as well as the specific binding capacity of Ky2 aptamer to kanamycin. Upon adding kanamycin antibiotic, the free aptamer sequences are firstly exhausted to form some complexes, which leads to AuNPs aggregation in high salt concentration. Consequently, the absorber’s absorption spectrum changes and no longer overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs, which results in obvious fluorescence recovery of the aptasensor. Herein, the effects of some vital parameters like the type and number of nanoparticles on the fluorescent aptasensor have been investigated. Under optimal conditions, the proposed aptasensor can detect kanamycin in a linear range of 0.04–0.24 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 18 nM. Moreover, the further studies also validate the applicability of the proposed aptasensor in milk samples, revealing that it may have enormous potential utility for practical kanamycin detection in food products in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent spectroscopy
Antibiotics
Aptasensor
Carbon dots
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the fluorescent aptasensor
Detection of kanamycin in milk samples
Sensing principle of the fluorescent aptasensor
Determination of kanamycin in spiked real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guizhou Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Emission spectra of the prepared CDs treated with different substances. 1. CDs alone; 2. CC-AuNPs + CDs; 3. CC-AuNPs + NaCI + CDs; 4. Ky2 aptamer + CC-AuNPs + CDs; 5. Ky2 aptamer + CC-AuNPs + NaCI + CDs; 6. Ky2 aptamer + CC-AuNPs + NaCI + CDs +0.2 μM kanamycin; 7. Ky2 aptamer + CC-AuNPs + NaCI + CDs +1 μM kanamycin. The amounts of CDs, NaCI, CC-AuNPs and Ky2 aptamer were 0.2 mg mL-1, 30 μM, 3.2 nM and 30 nM, respectively. The excitation and emission wavelength were 320 nm and 430 nm.

(a) TEM images of CDs-1. (b) Size distribution histogram of CDs-1. (c) Excitation and emission spectra as well as UV-vis absorption spectrum of CDs-1. Inset shows the photographs of CDs-1 solution under visible light (left) and UV lamp light (right). (d) Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of CDs-1 recorded at various excitation wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm.

Effect of absorber on the fluorescent aptasensor. (a) The fluorescence intensities of the prepared CDs treated with different types of AuNPs and other substances; (b) Effect of different amounts of CC-AuNPs on the fluorescent aptasensor. The excitation and emission wavelength were 320 nm and 430 nm.

Sensitivity of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor for kanamycin detection. (a) Fluorescence emission of the aptasensor treated with various amount of kanamycin under the irradiation of ultraviolet light. (b) The fluorescence spectra of the aptasensor treated with varying concentrations of kanamycin. (c) Calibration curve of the fluorescence intensity variations versus the amounts of kanamycin. Inset: fluorescence intensity variations of the aptasensor at low concentrations of kanamycin.

Selectivity of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor for kanamycin detection. (a) Fluorescence emission of the aptasensor treated with various competitive antibiotics under the irradiation of ultraviolet light. Samples 1–8 are kanamycin, diclofenac, oxytetracycline, penicillin, amoxicillin, carbamazepine, tetracycline, streptomycin, respectively. (b) Selectivity of the present fluorescent aptasensor for kanamycin detection. The concentrations of competitive antibiotics and kanamycin were all 1 μM.
Schematic representation of the fluorescent aptamer sensor for kanamycin detection based on internal filter effect.
Determination of kanamycin in spiked real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Milk was purchased from local market.

head.ce:title: A label-free and carbon dots based fluorescent aptasensor for the detection of kanamycin in milk

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Liquor and Food Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel label-free aptasensor for kanamycin detection was constructed using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as absorber to quench the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) via the inner filter effect (IFE). The strategy was mainly relied on the fact that the absorption spectra of AuNPs overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectra of fluorophores as well as the specific binding capacity of Ky2 aptamer to kanamycin. Upon adding kanamycin antibiotic, the free aptamer sequences are firstly exhausted to form some complexes, which leads to AuNPs aggregation in high salt concentration. Consequently, the absorber’s absorption spectrum changes and no longer overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs, which results in obvious fluorescence recovery of the aptasensor. Herein, the effects of some vital parameters like the type and number of nanoparticles on the fluorescent aptasensor have been investigated. Under optimal conditions, the proposed aptasensor can detect kanamycin in a linear range of 0.04–0.24 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 18 nM. Moreover, the further studies also validate the applicability of the proposed aptasensor in milk samples, revealing that it may have enormous potential utility for practical kanamycin detection in food products in the future.

A novel label-free aptasensor based on fluorescent spectroscopy assay was constructed for kanamycin detection using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as absorber to quench the fluorescence of carbon dots (CDs) via the inner filter effect.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Kanamycin, as an aminoglycoside bactericidal antibiotic, has been widely used in veterinary medicine to treat ovinia, mastitis, pneumonia, bacillary diarrhea and other infections [1,2]. However, the excess utilization of kanamycin could result in presence of its residues in animal derived food products, causing serious side effects to human health such as tinnitus and loss of hearing, respiratory failure, toxicity to kidneys and allergic reactions [3,4]. Therefore, many countries and regions have set a standard for kanamycin residues in animal products. The European Commission stipulates the maximum residue limit of kanamycin in milk and meat as 150 and 100 μg kg-1, respectively. Meanwhile, a similar standard is also established by the Ministry of Agriculture of China [5]. To achieve the detection of residual kanamycin in food samples, a variety of traditional methods including liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [6], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8,9], and the surface plasmon resonance [10] have been established. Although these methods can efficiently detect kanamycin, there are still some hindrances including the time-consuming procedures, susceptibility to interferences, difficult operation and the requirement of expensive apparatus [11]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a rapid and cost-effective method for the detection of kanamycin.

Aptamer is a short single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) or RNA sequence that selected from the random nucleic acid libraries [12]. Compared to natural recognition molecules like antibodies and enzymes, aptamer not only possess great binding ability with high specificity, sensitivity and remarkable affinity, but also offer numerous superiorities such as cost-effectiveness, simplicity of synthesis and modification, low toxicity and immunogenicity [13]. Ban and coworkers have screened out an ssDNA aptamer sequence (named Ky2) against kanamycin with a dissociation constant (Kd) of 78.8 nM, and they further employed such aptamer to detect kanamycin with LOD of 25 nM [14]. Subsequently, a variety of fluorometric aptasensors were developed for the detection of kanamycin using some fluorophores labelled aptamer as a specific recognition element [15,16]. Although these aptasensors possess excellent sensitivity and selectivity, the process of fluorescent signal modification is relative tediousness and difficulty. Moreover, the fluorescence signals of metallic and organic dyes in aptasensors will limit their roles in the fields of biological and food analysis. Thus, an environmental-friendly and label-free fluorescent method for kanamycin detection is still expected.

The inner filter effect (IFE) based fluorescent methods have attracted much attentions in recent years, which do not need covalent linking between fluorophore and absorber in the analytical detection [17]. This strategy requires spectral overlapping between the absorption of the absorbers and the fluorescence excitation or emission of fluorophores [18]. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) as an novel fluorophore that have been widespread applied in the fields of catalysis [19], cell imaging [20] and analytical detection [21,22], which owing to their outstanding performance like unique photoluminescence properties, excellent biocompatibility, high quantum yield, as well as chemical stability and environmental friendliness [23,24]. Recently, we have developed a novel fluorescent aptasensor for acetamiprid detection based on IFE between AuNPs and CDs [25]. Unfortunately, the quenching efficiency of AuNPs toward CDs is only 50%, which lead to a relatively high fluorescence background in the proposed aptasensor. The IFE based aptasensor is simple and flexible in the fabrication with high sensitivity and selectivity. To our best knowledge, the similar strategy for kanamycin antibiotic detection has still not been given enough consideration.

Herein, we prepared the CDs with higher quenching efficiency respond to citrate-coated gold nanoparticles (CC-AuNPs), and firstly adopted the IFE strategy to design a label-free fluorescent aptasensor for Kanamycin detection. The concept and process of the aptasensor are showed in Scheme 1 . In the absence of kanamycin, the Ky2 sequence can adsorb on the surface of AuNPs and enable their dispersion state, which can effectively quench the fluorescence of the CDs. In the addition of kanamycin, the Ky2 aptamer sequences are firstly exhausted to form some complexes, which lead to the aggregation of the AuNPs in high salt concentration. Thus, the proposed aptasensor displays its fluorescence signal because the aggregated AuNPs change their absorption spectrum and no longer quench the fluorescence of the CDs. The fluorescence intensity of the proposed aptasensor mainly depends on the status of the absorber, which is in turn conditioned directly by the concentration of kanamycin molecules. Therefore, this aptasensor enables to detect kanamycin by fluorescence assay, and may be extended to detect other biological and environmental samples by replacing corresponding aptamers.

Antibiotics including kanamycin, diclofenac, oxytetracycline, penicillin, amoxicillin and so on were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). The kanamycin specific-binding sequences (Ky2 aptamer) were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China), and its sequence is 5′-TGGGGGTTGAGGCTAAGCCGA-3’. Before use, the Ky2 aptamer sequence was dissolved in buffer solution (10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0) and drastically denatured at 90 °C for 5 min, then cooled to room temperature for the following binding. Unless otherwise mentioned, all reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification or treatment, and bi-distilled water was used throughout.

The fluorescence intensity was record on F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the excited slit of 5 nm and emission slit of 10 nm, as well as the photomultiplier voltage of 400 V. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was employed to characterize the morphology and size of nanoparticles. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The UV-vis absorption spectra were obtained using UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The zeta potentials of nanoparticles were determined on a Nano Particle & zeta potential Analyzer (Beckman Coulter, USA).

The CDs were prepared according to the previously reported method [26]. Briefly, citric acid (0.6 g) and formamide (2 mL) were dissolved in 18 mL of bi-distilled water. The solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 190 °C for 3 h. After the reaction in Teflon-lined autoclave, the unreacted substances and some other small molecules formed were removed from the mixture by dialysis (MWCO 500) and rotational evaporation. Finally, the solution was dried into a solid in a vacuum drying oven and then made into 2 mg mL-1 stock solution. The quantum yield of as-prepared CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as reference [27]. Preparation of different CDs and their properties are summarized in Table S1. In addition, different types of AuNPs were prepared and their concentrations were determined according to our previously reported method [28].

In a typical experiment, 10 μL of certain concentration of kanamycin stock solutions was mixed thoroughly with 10 μL of kanamycin aptamer solution (30 nM) in a 1.5 mL plastic tube and incubated at 30 °C for 10 min. Next, 100 μL of AuNPs was added into the above tube, and the mixture was incubated at 30 °C for another 10 min. Subsequently, 30 mL of 30 μM NaCI solution was added to the plastic tube, and the mixture was incubated at 30 °C for another 30 min. Then 20 μL of the CD solution (0.2 mg/mL) was added to the above mixed solutions and reacted for 10 min at 30 °C. Finally, an appropriate volume of the bi-distilled water was used to give a final volume of 250 μL, after which the above samples were moved to a quartz cell of 0.75 mL, and the fluorescence measurements were performed. The fluorescence intensities of the aptasensor in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of kanamycin were collected, and the fluorescent recovery efficiency (ΔF=F-F0) was calculated to evaluate the performance of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor for kanamycin detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the fluorescent aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal sensing conditions, a varying amount of kanamycin with final concentrations ranging from 0.04 μM to 2 μM were added into the proposed fluorescent aptasensor to determine LOD following the procedure of kanamycin determination. To investigate selectivity of the fluorometric aptasensor, some antibiotics widely used in animal husbandry including diclofenac, oxytetracycline, penicillin, amoxicillin, carbamazepine, tetracycline and streptomycin were added to the sensing solutions and their fluorescent intensities were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of kanamycin in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milks were bought from local supermarkets and chosen as the real samples for kanamycin analysis. Before detection, the milk samples were spiked with quantitively amount of kanamycin and were briefly pretreated to ensure transparency [5]. Firstly, the milk samples were dropwise added with 20% acetic acid to adjust the pH to 4.6. Then they were incubated at 45 °C for 10 min to precipitate casein, followed by the centrifugal removal of proteins and fats at 10000 rpm for 25 min. Finally, all samples were filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane and the filtrates were adjusted to neutral pH for further analysis. The detection of kanamycin in milk samples was validated by the designed fluorescent aptasensor as described above. Moreover, the operating parameters of LC-MS method for kanamycin assay was referred to previous report [29], which was employed to validate the reliability of the fluorescent aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing principle of the fluorescent aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To test the validity of the assay for the detection of kanamycin, the fluorescent spectra of the proposed aptasensor were recorded under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 1 , the pure CDs solution displays a significant characteristic fluorescence peak at 430 nm (curve 1) and emits bright blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet light (sample 1 in the inset photo). After adding a certain amount of quencher (CC-AuNPs), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs sharply decreased to 320 and the quenching efficiency reached 65% (curve 2), which is higher than that of previous CDs (50%) [25]. The curve 3 demonstrated that a certain concentration of NaCI salt could aggregate AuNPs, and the solution showed bright blue fluorescence (sample 3 in the inset photo). The curves 4–5 reveal that absorber nanoparticles wrapped with aptamers can effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs even in high amount salt. Upon adding kanamycin, the fluorescence intensity of aptasensor recovery, and its degree is proportional to the amount of kanamycin (sample 6–7 in the inset photo). Therefore, the proposed aptasensor enables the detection of kanamycin though the fluorescence assay.

To further determine the possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching, we recorded the absorption spectra of dispersed and aggregated AuNPs, then integrated them with the excitation and emission spectra of the CDs in Fig. S1. The dispersed AuNPs have a strong absorption value from 400 nm to 600 nm and reach a maximum absorption peak near at 525 nm, which almost overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs. This evidence reveals that the dispersed AuNPs can effectively quench the fluorescence of the CDs through the IFE.

To obtain excellent performance of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor, some experimental parameters including kinds of CDs, concentration of salt solution, absorber conditions as well as aptamer amounts were investigated.

The morphology of CDs was investigated by TEM, which confirms that CDs-1 possess a well monodisperse morphology (Fig. 2 a). The particle size distribution of CDs-1 was determined using a certain mount of particles, and the results show that their average particle size is about 3.5 nm (Fig. 2b). In addition, we recorded the optical performance of the CDs-1 in Fig. 2c, the UV-vis performance of CDs-1 shows a strong characteristic absorption peak at around 320 nm, which can be ascribed to the n–π* (C=O) transitions [30]. The fluorescence spectra of CDs-1 show a maximum emission intensity at 430 nm. The CDs-1 solution exhibits a transparent appearance under sunlight and bright blue fluorescence emission under UV light at 365 nm (inset). The emission wavelengths under different excitation wavelengths are displayed in detail in Fig. 2d. The fluorescence emission bands of the as-prepared CDs-1 occurred 30 nm red shift when excitation wavelengths were applied in the range of 300 to 400 nm, indicating that CDs-1 possess distinctively excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior [31].

Surface functional groups of the CDs-1 was provided by FTIR characterization (Fig. S2). The FTIR spectra show high-intensity peaks at 3439 and 2928 cm-1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of N-H and C-H, respectively. The absorption peak appears at 2112 cm-1 is associated with the stretching vibration of -N=C=O, which may be provided by formamide in the synthesized CDs-1. The absorption at around 1248 cm-1 is related to the symmetric bending vibration of C-NH-C. Understanding the structure of CDs-1 are of great significance for its application.

The characteristics of CDs are strongly related to the size and surface properties of nanoparticles [32]. Herein, we investigated the effects of several carbon dots (named CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3) on the current fluorescent aptasensor for kanamycin detection. As presented in Fig. S3, all CDs possess the capacity to fabricate fluorescent aptasensor. Surprisingly, the quenched efficiency of CDs-1 (65%) is higher than that of CDs-2 (50%) and CDs-3 (46%) respond to the same amount of absorber. Meanwhile, the fluorescence recovery efficiency of CDs-1 based aptasensor was the most remarkable in the presence of kanamycin antibiotic. Thus, CDs-1 is an appropriate fluorescent material to fabricate the aptasensor in the subsequent experiments.

Usually, the AuNPs is tend aggregation in high salt concentration [14]. Accordingly, salt ions concentration is one of important parameter for the fluorescent aptasensor. As demonstrated in Fig. S4, the F and F0 of CDs solution shows a gradual enhance trend with the increasing amount of salt, which is attributed to the change of AuNPs status. When the concentration of NaCI is 30 μM, the fluorescent recovery efficiency (ΔF=F-F0) has reached the maximum value of 350. Therefore, 30 μM of NaCI was applied in the subsequent experiments.

The zeta potential of CC-AuNPs, cysteamine-stabilized AuNPs (CS-AuNPs) and unmodified AuNPs (Um-AuNPs) were measured to be -3.78 mV, 11.20 mV, and -10.38 mV [28], which were individually employed to construct the fluorescent aptasensors for kanamycin detection. As shown in Fig. 3 a, all three kinds of AuNPs could quench CDs fluorescence emission. CC-AuNPs could efficiently quench CDs fluorescence and its quenching ability was stronger than that of the same charged Um-AuNPs, revealing that the CC-AuNPs prepared aptasensor has a relatively low background signal, and its fluorescence recovery was remarkable in the addition of 1 μM kanamycin antibiotic. The positively charged CS-AuNPs could efficiently quench CDs fluorescence via the strong electrostatic attraction effect, but the absorber nanoparticles inevitably aggregated under the cover of negatively charged Ky2 aptamer sequences, which reduced fluorescence quenching efficiency to CDs and made aptasensor present a higher background sensing signal. Accordingly, the fluorescence recovery of aptasensor was extremely weak after the addition of kanamycin antibiotic. In contrast, the quenching ability of Um-AuNPs was slight and its fluorescent recovery was negligible. Thus, CC-AuNPs is an appropriate absorber to fabricate fluorescent aptasensor.

To achieve good performance in the detection of kanamycin, the concentration of CC-AuNPs ranging from 1.28 nM to 4.48 nM were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3b, lower concentration of CC-AuNPs is not enough to fully quench the fluorescence of CDs, so the aptasensor present a high fluorescence background signal of aptasensor, which result in the ΔF is lower. With the increase in the amount of absorber, the background signal of the aptasensor decreased while the degree of fluorescence recovery increased. Once the concentration of CC-AuNPs exceeded 3.2 nM, the excess absorber would further quench the fluorescence of CDs, the fluorescence recovery of aptasensor showed a downward trend. Therefore, 3.2 nM of CC-AuNPs was employed in the subsequent experiments.

Aptamer acts as the specific target binding molecule in the design of assay, so its concentration was crucial for the detection of kanamycin. As can be seen from Fig. S5, lower concentration of Ky2 aptamer was inadequate to wrap absorber nanoparticles, so that most of AuNPs could aggregate under a relatively high amount of salt and resulted in a high fluorescence background signal of aptasensor. Noticeably, the fluorescence recovery reached the maximum value of 370 when the concentration of absorber was 30 nM. A certain number of kanamycin was insufficient to bind with the excess aptamer sequences if the amount of Ky2 aptamer was over 30 nM. Accordingly, the excess sequences would wrap absorber nanoparticles and further enhance the quenching capacity of AuNPs, resulting in a relatively low fluorescence recovery of aptasensor. Therefore, the amount of aptamer was used as 30 nM in the subsequent experiments.

Under optimum assay conditions, the sensitivity of the proposed aptasensor was investigated by recording the fluorescence intensity with varying amount of kanamycin. It can be observed from Fig. 4 a that the fluorescence emission of plastic tub was enhanced gradually with increasing concentration of kanamycin from 0.04 to 2 μM. Accordingly, the fluorescent intensity of aptasensor was recovered gradually (Fig. 4b). Based on the calibration plot of fluorescence recovery (ΔF) with kanamycin concentration, an excellent linear relationship was obtained in the range of 0.04–0.24 μM with the calibration curve (Fig. 4c) was sigmoidal in shape. The linear equation can be expressed as y = 0.17x + 8.11 with a correlation coefficient of 0.998. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 18 nM, which was calculated according to the 3σ/slope [33]. Furthermore, the performance of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor was compared with existing other aptamer-based methods (Table S2). Although some part of these methods possesses the wider liner range and lower LOD values than the proposed assay, they still face some drawbacks. Electrochemical aptasensors usually need relatively complicated modification of electrodes, and the determination should be completed on the electrochemical workstation, which is unbeneficial to their large-scale application in practical samples [4,34]. The reported colorimetric aptasensor using AgNPs as a signal element depends on the variation of yellow color in solution, whose characteristic absorption peak is close to the ultraviolet region, so it is difficult to distinguish the subtle change of the color by naked eyes, which will hamper its extension in real-time and on-site practical applications [35]. The reported fluorescent aptasensors using organic dyes or heavy metals are usually harmful to the environment and human health [36]. In contrast, the proposed aptasensor employed non-toxic CDs as the sensing material, and did not require any modification of aptamer sequences or nanoparticles in the process of aptasensor fabrication, so it can be applied to the real-time and convenient detection of kanamycin residues in real samples if combined with a portable ultraviolet lamp.

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor, some competitive antibiotics such as diclofenac, oxytetracycline, penicillin, amoxicillin, carbamazepine, tetracycline and streptomycin were individually added to the aptasensor. The corresponding visual images of each sample are shown in Fig. 5 a, and it is noteworthy that only the addition of kanamycin into the proposed aptasensor could emit a bright blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet light. As shown in Fig. 5b, the F0 of aptasensor with 1 μM of other competitive antibiotics were far lower than that with an equal amount of kanamycin, and their values increased sharply after appending the certain number of kanamycin. These results clearly indicate that the proposed aptasensor exhibits excellent selectivity and other competitive antibiotics display negligible interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of kanamycin in spiked real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the viability of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor in practical application, a series concentration of kanamycin was spiked into two milk samples, and the results are summarized in Table 1 . It could be found that the spiked recoveries of the proposed fluorescent aptasensor for kanamycin were in the range of 90.0 to 98.3%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be 1.86–4.21%. Compared with the measurement of LC-MS, the results reveal that our sensing strategy has good practical applicability and can be potentially used as an alternative approach to detect kanamycin in real samples.

In summary, a novel label-free aptasensor based on fluorescent spectroscopy assay have been developed for the detection of kanamycin residues in milks via IFE of AuNPs toward CDs. In the presence of kanamycin, the fluorescence intensity of aptasensor can recover and the variation is directly associated with the amount of kanamycin antibiotic. The proposed aptasensor not only possess excellent selectivity against other competitive antibiotics, but also achieve highly sensitive quantitative detection of kanamycin with a LOD of 18 nM. Moreover, the fluorescent aptasensor was further applied to real samples detection, and the recovery rate was determined from 90.0% to 98.3%, which make its potential application for kanamycin detection in practical samples. If substitute other specific aptamer as recognition molecule, the proposed aptasensor may offer alternative approach to detect some other target of interest in food and the environment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31760486, 21565009); the Natural Science Foundation of Guizhou Province ([2016]1403); the Science and Technology Support Program of Guizhou Province for Social Development ([2018]2795, [2018]2805); the Science and Technology Program of Guizhou Province for Talent Team Building ([2018]5781, [2017]5788); the Construction Program of Biology First-class Discipline in Guizhou (GNYL[2017]009); the Foundation of Key Laboratory of Wuliangye-flavor Liquor Solid-state Fermentation (2018JJ002), China National Light Industry; and the Foundation of Key Laboratory of Urban Agriculture (UA201701), Ministry of Agriculture, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117651.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficacy of carbonaceous nanocomposites for sorbing ionizable antibiotic sulfamethazine from aqueous solution
Water Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhang, C. Lai, G.M. Zeng, D.L. Huang, C.P. Yang, Y. Wang, Y.Y. Zhou, M. Cheng, Efficacy of carbonaceous nanocomposites for sorbing ionizable antibiotic sulfamethazine from aqueous solution, Water Res., 95 (2016) 103-112.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectrochemical aptasensing of kanamycin using visible light-activated carbon nitride and graphene oxide nanocomposites
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.Z. Li, Y. Liu, L. Cheng, C.Z. Yang, J.D. Zhang, Photoelectrochemical Aptasensing of Kanamycin Using Visible Light-Activated Carbon Nitride and Graphene Oxide Nanocomposites, Anal. Chem., 86 (2014) 9372-9375.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Extremely sensitive sandwich assay of kanamycin using surface-enhanced Raman scattering of 2-mercaptobenzothiazole labeled gold@silver nanoparticles
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Zengin, U. Tamer, T. Caykara, Extremely sensitive sandwich assay of kanamycin using surface-enhanced Raman scattering of 2-mercaptobenzothiazole labeled gold@silver nanoparticles, Anal. Chim. Acta, 817 (2014) 33-41.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free detection of kanamycin based on the aptamer-functionalized conducting polymer/gold nanocomposite
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhu, P. Chandra, K.M. Song, C. Ban, Y.B. Shim, Label-free detection of kanamycin based on the aptamer-functionalized conducting polymer/gold nanocomposite, Biosens. Bioelectron., 36 (2012) 29-34.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer based fluorometric determination of kanamycin using double-stranded DNA and carbon nanotubes
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.G. Liao, B.H. Wei, L.G. Luo, Aptamer based fluorometric determination of kanamycin using double-stranded DNA and carbon nanotubes, Microchim. Acta, 184 (2017) 627-632.

Chemosphere

Zmudzki, Liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry multiclass method for the determination of antibiotics residues in water samples from water supply systems in food-producing animal farms, Chemosphere, 119 (2015) 8-15.
